data.title: Citigroup Says Odds for Weaker Brent Crude Prices in 2nd Half of 2025 Have Improved — OPIS
data.description:
Citigroup on Wednesday said it believes the likelihood that Brent crude oil prices will fall to a $60 to $65/bbl range by the second half of this year has improved.

The bank said it continues to believe non-OPEC+ supply growth will exceed global demand growth that could be suppressed by tariffs. It also sees the oil market moving to a surplus in the last three quarters of 2025.

In addition, Citi said it believes Iran's oil exports will fall by 200,000 to 300,000 b/d. It added, however, that recent comments by President Trump suggest the U.S. may be willing to renew negotiations with Iran. The bank said Iranian President Masoud Pezeshkian appears to recognize potential diplomatic solutions that could ease sanctions.

The wildcard for Iranian oil flows may be China, which is the largest buyer of Iranian oil. Citi said that while China recently balked at purchasing Iranian crude, a return to previous volumes could limit the effect of sanctions.

Iranian oil can be re-exported from Iraq's port of Basra or refined into harder-to-track refined products' exports, it said.

Citi also suggested the White House may act to increase U.S. capital expenditures following the Senate confirmation of oil industry executive Chris Wright as energy secretary. The report said every 1 million b/d increase in U.S. output could cost between $3.5 billion and $5 billion.

This content was created by Oil Price Information Service, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. OPIS is run independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

  • Reporting by Tom Kloza, tkloza@opisnet.com; Editing by Jeff Barber, jbarber@opisnet.com

data.title: UGI CORP /PA/ SEC 10-Q Report
data.description:
UGI Corporation, a diversified energy services company with operations in the United States and Europe, has released its Form 10-Q report for the first quarter of fiscal year 2024. The report highlights the company's robust financial performance and operational efficiency, despite a slight decline in revenues. The company continues to focus on optimizing its existing operations and strategic initiatives to drive future growth.

Financial Highlights

  • Revenues: $2,030 million, reflecting a decrease from $2,121 million in the prior year period, primarily due to lower sales volumes.
  • Operating Income: $487 million, a significant increase from $232 million in the prior year period, driven by improved cost management and efficiency gains.
  • Net Income Attributable to UGI Corporation: $375 million, up from $94 million in the prior year period, indicating strong overall financial performance.
  • Basic Earnings Per Common Share: $1.74, compared to $0.45 in the prior year period, highlighting improved profitability.
  • Diluted Earnings Per Common Share: $1.74, compared to $0.44 in the prior year period, reflecting the company's enhanced earnings capacity.

Business Highlights

  • Revenue Segments: UGI Corporation's revenue is segmented across various business lines, including Utilities, Midstream & Marketing, UGI International, and AmeriGas Propane. Utilities generated $470 million, Midstream & Marketing $296 million, UGI International $638 million, and AmeriGas Propane $627 million in revenue for the three months ended December 31, 2024.
  • Geographical Performance: UGI International, which operates primarily in Europe, reported revenues of $638 million, reflecting its significant presence in the LPG distribution market across the continent. The segment's performance is influenced by regional market conditions and currency fluctuations.
  • Sales Units: The company reported significant sales volumes in its LPG segment, with 410 million gallons of LPG swaps under contract as of December 31, 2024. This indicates a robust demand for LPG products across its operational regions.
  • Future Outlook: Management anticipates continued growth in the Utilities segment, driven by ongoing system improvements and regulatory approvals for rate increases. The company is also focused on strategic initiatives to enhance operational efficiency and expand its customer base across all segments.

SEC Filing: UGI CORP /PA/ [ UGI ] - 10-Q - Feb. 06, 2025

data.title: Clean Core Thorium Energy Raises $15.5M in Series Seed
data.description:
CHICAGO, Feb. 06, 2025 (GLOBE NEWSWIRE) — Clean Core Thorium Energy (CCTE) has raised a $15.5M Series Seed round of financing to advance the ANEEL fuel - a patented blend of thorium & HALEU designed to be seamlessly deployed into existing reactors. With plans to commercialize in 2026, the capital will be used towards solidifying the company’s technical partnerships, such as the irradiation test at Idaho National Laboratory (INL), the further validation work at Canadian Nuclear Laboratories (CNL), a planned Demonstration Irradiation, and more. Further, this financing will be used to fund activities related to the company’s hiring, supply chain partnerships, and regulatory and licensing requirements.

The syndicate of investors participating in this round are led by a Singapore-based family office and notable business leaders including Sumant Sinha, CEO and Founder of ReNew, Lakshmi Narayanan, former CEO of Cognizant Technologies, and Deepak Parekh, former Chairman of HDFC Bank.

Mehul Shah, CCTE’s CEO and Founder, said:

“We are not only excited to have fresh capital infused in the business for critical growth, but also ecstatic to invite those with deep ties to the global energy industry as advisors and investors to accelerate our progress. This announcement marks the next chapter in CCTE’s growth to revolutionize nuclear energy.

By demonstrating thorium’s ability to deliver immediate and substantial operational benefits, we can accelerate the renewed momentum of nuclear. This new fuel cycle enhances reactor economics, strengthens energy security, improves safety, and ensures proliferation resistance—all within an existing and proven reactor system."

In 2024, CCTE achieved major milestones to advance the ANEEL fuel, including:

  • The high burn-up irradiation test at INL achieved up to 25 GWd/ton, demonstrating exceptional performance and achieving a historic milestone for thorium
  • Canadian Nuclear Safety Commission (CNSC), the global regulator for CANDUs, completed a successful Phase 1 pre-licensing Vendor Design Review of ANEEL fuel
  • Larsen & Toubro (L&T) and National Thermal Power Corporation (NTPC) signed strategic partnerships to advance the ANEEL fuel

About Clean Core Thorium Energy

Clean Core Thorium Energy is a nuclear fuel company exploring thorium-driven nuclear innovations. Clean Core’s patented nuclear fuel technology (called the ANEEL™ fuel) is comprised of thorium and high assay low-enriched uranium (HALEU) and is capable of improving the safety and cost-efficiency of pressurized heavy-water reactors. The ANEEL™ fuel is a novel solution to safety, waste, and proliferation concerns in today’s nuclear plants. Learn more at https://cleancore.energy/. Follow us on social media: LinkedIn and X.

Contact: info@cleancore.energy

data.title: Gold halts record rally, drops 1% as US jobs report looms
data.description:
Key points:
  • Gold hit a record high of $2,882.16 on Wednesday
  • Markets await US non-farm payrolls report due on Friday
  • Gold's RSI indicates overbought conditions
  • BoE's gold stock down 2%, says Deputy Governor Ramsden

Gold prices slid 1% on Thursday as the U.S. dollar firmed ahead of a key jobs report and investors booked profits, after bullion recorded consecutive record peaks in the previous five sessions on the back of escalating trade tensions between U.S. and China.

Spot gold slipped 1% to $2,835.31 per ounce by 10:09 a.m. ET (1509 GMT) after hitting an all-time high of $2,882.16 on Wednesday. U.S. gold futures dropped 1.3% to $2,856.30.

"There's probably a combination of a stronger dollar, some profit taking and yields moving a little bit higher off their lows," weighing on gold ahead of the U.S. employment report, said Daniel Pavilonis, senior market strategist at RJO Futures.

Nonfarm payrolls likely increased by 170,000 jobs after surging to 256,000 in December, a Reuters survey of economists showed. The unemployment rate is forecast unchanged at 4.1%.

A resilient labor market is fuelling economic growth and allowing the Federal Reserve to halt interest rate cuts as it evaluates the inflationary effects of Trump's fiscal, trade, and immigration policies.

"In addition to the volatility in general, we still have inflation in the background that's starting to creep up, so gold is responding as a safe haven," said Alex Ebkarian, chief operating officer at Allegiance Gold.

"Gold is on its way for $2,900, and you have very strong sentiment despite the fact that in the short term dollar gained strength."

On a technical basis, gold's Relative Strength Index (RSI) stands above 70, indicating that the metal is overbought.

Meanwhile, the stock of gold at the Bank of England has fallen by about 2% since the end of last year, its Deputy Governor Dave Ramsden said, citing strong demand for gold stored at the bank to take advantage of international price differentials.

In other metals, spot silver dropped 1.7% to $31.76 per ounce, and palladium fell 1.8% to $971.25. Platinum fell 0.1% to $978.10.

data.title: Mastercraft Boat Shares Rise After Surprise 2Q Profit
data.description:
By Chris Wack

Mastercraft Boat Holdings shares were up 11% to $20.29 after the company reported a profit for the fiscal second quarter, instead of the loss analysts were expecting.

The Vonore, Tenn., recreational powerboat maker said it had quarterly earnings of $2.7 million, or 17 cents a share, compared with $5.9 million, or 35 cents a share, in the same quarter a year earlier. Analysts polled by FactSet were looking for a loss of $600,000, or 4 cents a share.

Sales for the quarter were $63.4 million, compared with last year's $89.8 million and the $60.8 million analysts were expecting.

Mastercraft said the decrease in net sales was primarily due to planned lower unit volumes, leading to lower dealer inventory levels, and an unfavorable model mix.

The company also said all debt amounts have been repaid, leaving $62.9 million of cash and investments, with $100 million of availability on its revolving credit facility.

Mastercraft is expecting fiscal 2025 sales of $275 million to $295 million, and adjusted earnings of 64 cents to 86 cents a share. Analysts are looking for 2025 sales of $291.4 million and adjusted earnings of 71 cents a share.

Write to Chris Wack at chris.wack@wsj.com

data.title: UK firms trim wage growth expectations, BoE survey shows
data.description:
British companies' expectations for wage growth fell marginally in January and expectations for employment growth also declined in part due to a looming increase in payroll taxes, a survey from the Bank of England showed on Thursday.

The Decision Maker Panel showed businesses expected wage growth of 3.9% over the coming year in the three months to January, 0.1 percentage points lower than in the three months to December.

The BoE's Monetary Policy Committee said earlier on Thursday it expected pay growth to slow, based on signals including from the DMP survey, as it announced a quarter-point cut to its benchmark Bank Rate to 4.5%.

Over half of firms contacted in the DMP survey expected to cut staff and 38% thought they would pay lower wages due to the increase in employers' social security contributions announced by finance minister Rachel Reeves in her budget in October and which is due to begin in April.

Almost two thirds expected to lower their profit margins and 56% said they would raise prices in response to the change.

Companies' expectations for consumer price inflation in the year ahead inched up slightly, rising by 0.2 percentage points to 3.0% in the three months to January.

Firms expected to increase their prices by 3.9% over the next year, up from 3.9% in the three months to December.

data.title: Graham CEO to Transition to Executive Chairman in June
data.description:
Graham said Thursday that President and Chief Executive Daniel Thoren will transition to the role of executive chairman, effective June 10.

The company said Thoren will remain active in the operations of the company for the foreseeable future. It also said that Thoren will serve as a strategic advisor.

Jonathan Painter, chairman of the board of Directors, will transition to lead independent director, Graham said.

The company also said it has appointed Matthew Malone as president and chief operating officer, effective Feb. 5, and will succeed Thoren as CEO in June. Malone served as the vice president and general manager of Graham's Barber-Nichols unit.

Graham said it reiterated its sales and adjusted EBITDA guidance as provided on Nov. 8.

data.title: Seasonal Bias in January Inflation: A Myth? — Market Talk
data.description:
1039 ET - In both 2023 and 2024, January inflation figures came in hotter than expected. Many economists, including some Fed officials, have argued that the pattern reflects how companies reset prices at the start of the year, and importantly, that the seasonal adjustment in government statistics has failed to account strongly enough for this trend. If so, January could bring hot inflation numbers again. Barclays analysts study the issue and conclude it's a "myth." For one thing, the January 2023 inflation surprise was led by goods prices, while in 2024, it was a services story, so the episodes seem distinct, Barclays writes. They also run a set of statistical tests on the monthly numbers, and deem the myth busted. January CPI figures are due Feb 12. (matt.grossman@wsj.com, @mattgrossman)

1027 ET - U.S. labor productivity continued a solid stretch of gains in 4Q, although with some deceleration. Productivity in 4Q was 1.6% better than a year earlier, down from a 2.1% 12-month improvement through 3Q. The figures were in line with consensus estimates but could decline in the near term, Pantheon Macro economists write. Business-equipment investment has been weak over the past year, and in a weaker labor market, companies might not have to push as hard to squeeze output gains from a fixed set of employees. On the other hand, the medium-term outlook is brighter as AI adoption broadens, promising to improve productivity through the second half of the decade. (matt.grossman@wsj.com, @mattgrossman)

1014 ET - President Trump's tariff threats against Canada has produced one positive outcome, says former Bank of Canada governor David Dodge. "Trump has done us a big favor, forcing us as Canadians to think much harder and invest much more in increasing our own productivity," Dodge tells a webinar hosted by Canadian lobby firm Global Public Affairs. Canadian productivity, and business investment, have been moribund for years, badly lagging the U.S. Dodge warns he doesn't expect Canada to escape U.S. tariffs, as the Trump administration is seeking new revenue to pay for tax cuts. To compete, Canadian firms must pick up the pace of capital spending, "to drive down your production cost and drive up the quality of your product which makes it indispensable." (paul.vieira@wsj.com; @paulvieira)

1006 ET - The Bank of England could implement fewer interest-rate cuts than markets expect due to concerns about U.K. inflation reaccelerating, Jamie Constable, chief market strategist at Singer Capital Markets says in a note. The BOE cut rates by 25 basis points to 4.50% Thursday, but it could struggle to cut further as it forecasts U.K. inflation will rise to 3.7% in the third quarter, Constable says. The BOE could have to switch to a greater focus on growth in order to reduce rates, he says. "The BOE's mandate needs to change from being one dimensional and focused on inflation by adding growth and jobs as it is too restrictive as it is." (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

1003 ET - Although global and domestic risks make U.K. government bonds, or gilts, less appealing in the near term, they look attractive over the long-term, BlackRock's Vivek Paul says in a note. The Bank of England is likely to cut interest rates at upcoming meetings, which makes "current yields broadly reasonable relative to where we think they'll be in five years," Paul says. However, for now markets need to watch for risks of embedded stagflation, he says. The Office for Budget Responsibility's report on U.K. finances due on March 26, and the government's reaction to the report are likely to influence gilt market movements, Paul says. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

0949 ET - Cocoa alternatives are in growing demand given high prices for the real thing, Hershey CEO Michele Buck said. Some companies feeling the pressure have switched to cocoa butter alternatives, she said, reducing demand in the global cocoa market. "We do that where possible, but we're pretty precious about the brands and what they stand for with consumers, " Buck said. (roshan.fernandez@wsj.com)

0947 ET - A speech by Bank of Canada Governor Tiff Macklem after the market closes will be eyed for possible freshened guidance. The speech, titled "Future Challenges for Monetary Policy in the Americas," will be closely watched for comments on heightened trade uncertainty, Scotiabank's Derek Holt says. "My hunch is that he may lean toward saying the damage has been done regardless of whether tariffs actually do arrive or not, but with mixed effects on the supply side (via reduced investment) and demand (exports)," the economist says in his morning report. (robb.stewart@wsj.com)

0938 ET - Jobless claims rebounded last week but still indicated a strong labor market, supporting a continued pause on interest rate cuts by the Fed, writes Gaurav Mallik, of Pallas Capital Advisors. With attention turning to tomorrow's employment report, Mallik says that as long as the economy added between 170,000 and 200,000 jobs in January, "the market should largely absorb this number with little volatility." Anything beyond these limits could spark a repricing of Fed expectations, which currently point to one or two rate cuts this year. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

0936 ET - Canada's restaurant industry wants Ottawa to make its tax holiday permanent. Restaurants Canada estimates foodservice sales will grow 3.7% in 1Q thanks to a boost from the federal government's two-month removal of tax on restaurant meals, booze and certain other purchases. But the not-for-profit industry body says restaurants still have economic headwinds, including from cuts to immigration, still low consumer confidence and the tariff dispute with the U.S. It estimates restaurant, caterer and bar sales will surpass C$100 billion in 2025, representing a 3.9% nominal increase over 2024. But adjusted for menu inflation, real sales are forecast to grow by 0.8% compared to relatively flat real sales in 2024, it says. (robb.stewart@wsj.com)

0924 ET - Industry outlooks for Europe and Switzerland look mixed, according to a survey of entrepreneurs released by UBS. As many as 52% of entrepreneurs in Europe and 50% in Switzerland are very or somewhat optimistic about the outlook for their industry over the next 12 months, the report says. In both Europe and Switzerland, companies are expected to invest in strategic acquisitions, IT spending, and personnel over the next 12 months, the study says. In Europe, investment into artificial intelligence is also anticipated, it says. (nina.kienle@wsj.com)

0921 ET - Sterling looks at risk of further falls after the Bank of England signalled less caution over cutting interest rates, TD Securities analysts say in a note. The BOE delivered a highly anticipated 25 basis-point rate cut Thursday but two members surprisingly backed a larger 50 basis-point reduction. Investors have now trimmed long sterling positions--bets on the currency rising--, the analysts say. However, sterling positioning remains "relatively optimistic" compared to non-dollar peers. This leaves it vulnerable to further declines. "Moreover, while U.K. does not seem to be in the obvious line of fire for Trump tariffs and can outperform the euro, it is a risk-sensitive currency and heightened uncertainty is likely to weigh on it versus the dollar." (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0919 ET - The Czech koruna stays higher after the Czech National Bank delivered a widely anticipated interest-rate cut. The CNB resumed its rate-cutting cycle by lowering its key two-week repo rate by 25 basis points to 3.75%. Policymakers recently suggested a more cautious approach to monetary policy easing, Capital Economics economist Nicholas Farr says in a note. That means rate cuts from here are likely to be spaced out, he says. However, the prospect of continued slow economic growth and lower inflation could see rates fall to 3.0% by year-end. That compares to market expectations of 3.25%. EUR/CZK falls 0.4% on the day to 25.1149, compared to 25.1330 before the decision.(renae.dyer@wsj.com)

data.title: Latam markets higher on easing tariff fears; Mexican peso flat ahead of decision
data.description:
Key points:
  • Trump says Israel would hand over Gaza after fighting
  • China challenges Trump tariffs at WTO
  • Mexico's interest rate decision due
  • MSCI Latam FX up 0.5%, stocks advance 0.7%

Most Latin American markets rose on Thursday as investors continued to await more clues on U.S. President Donald Trump's stance on Gaza and a trade war with China.

MSCI's index tracking Latin American currencies (.MILA00000CUS) was 0.5% higher, while the stocks measure (.MILA00000PUS) was up 0.7%, trading at its highest level since early December.

Investors were monitoring geopolitical conflicts, for any Trump-caused developments.

Israel's shekel and dollar bonds in the country and its neighbors were little changed.

Russia's rouble rallied, while Ukraine's dollar bonds inched up over 1 cent each, on hopes of a conclusion to the conflict after the countries said they were in touch with the U.S. administration.

In Latin America, the Mexican peso was up 0.1% ahead of a monetary policy decision where the central bank is widely expected to deliver a jumbo 50 basis point rate cut.

"We assess a higher than market pricing probability of a smaller 25bps rate cut given lingering uncertainty and the scar-tissue left by the US announcement of tariffs on Mexico, and subsequent decision to delay (hold) implementation," analysts at Goldman Sachs said.

"Overall, the risk-reward of accelerating the pace of rate cuts at this meeting is not favorable."

An official on Wednesday said that Mexico and the U.S. had agreed to economic dialogue in a week riddled with changing stances on tariffs and volatile moves in exposed economies.

China challenged the duties and Trump's cancellation of a duty-free exemption for low-value packages at the World Trade Organization.

On the day, however, markets looked past risks of a global trade war, and looked for any signs of de-escalating trade tensions between the U.S. and China.

Stocks in Mexico were up 0.7%, aided by an over 5% boost in Cemex after the cement maker posted a net profit in the fourth quarter and rolled out a $350 million savings program.

Higher oil prices also helped boost currencies in Latin America, with the Colombian peso (COL=) up 0.5%, while its stocks (.COLCAP) were up 0.3%.

Copper prices hitting multi-week highs lifted the Chilean peso to a near three month high.

The Brazilian real was up 0.2%, after snapping a 12-session win streak on Wednesday.

Elsewhere, the Czech central bank resumed its interest rate-cutting cycle, lowering the main two-week repo rate by 25 basis points.

Highlights:

** Trump's aid cuts imperil emerging market investment cash

** Ecuador banana heir Noboa seeks full presidential term to push on with crime crackdown

** Zimbabwe central bank keeps policy rate at 35%

data.title: US Natural Gas Stocks Posts Larger Decline Than Expected in Week Ended Jan. 31
data.description:
US natural gas stocks fell by 174 billion cubic feet in the week ended Jan. 31, below the smaller decrease of 171 billion expected in a survey compiled by Bloomberg as of 7:35 am ET and following a decrease of 321 billion cubic feet in the previous week.

Stocks at 2.397 trillion cubic feet are 8% lower than in the comparable week a year ago and 4.4% below their 5-year average.

data.title: Jefferies Adjusts Old Dominion Freight Line Price Target to $195 From $190
data.description: Old Dominion Freight Line has an average rating of Hold and mean price target of $200.74, according to analysts polled by FactSet.

data.title: Insurer Markel jumps on strategic review announcement
data.description:
** Shares of insurer Markel Group jump as much as 7.9%; last up 6.7% at $1,984.11

** MKL late Wednesday announced a business review of its operations focused on the performance of its specialty insurance business

** Move comes after activist investor JANA Partners pushed for changes at the company

** Insurance is at the heart of what we do, and we're fully committed to supporting areas within insurance that are excelling while also addressing underperformance - MKL

** Co will also consider ways to simplify its structure as part of the review

** MKL shares jumped 21.6% in 2024

data.title: Update: Market Chatter: HSBC Said Preparing Over GBP15 Million Remuneration Package for CEO
data.description:
(Updates to include HSBC's response)

HSBC is reportedly consulting with shareholders and finalizing a maximum pay package worth over 15 million pounds sterling for Chief Executive Officer Georges Elhedery.

Sky News reported Thursday, citing unnamed sources, that the potential remuneration deal will reduce the chief executive's fixed salary by half but will significantly boost his maximum variable pay. It will also remove a fixed allowance but bonus and long-term share awards will have higher multiples.

HSBC said in response to a request for comment from MT Newswires that no decisions have been taken and that details will be released with its annual results report on Feb. 19.

(Market Chatter news is derived from conversations with market professionals globally. This information is believed to be from reliable sources but may include rumor and speculation. Accuracy is not guaranteed.)
